results.possibleIdentity: Richard Hollerith Jr. (1926–2022) was an American industrial designer and technical consultant based in Upper Montclair, New Jersey. He was the grandson of Herman Hollerith, the inventor of the punch card tabulating system that founded IBM. After serving in the Navy V-12 program and graduating from Dartmouth College in 1947 with degrees in Geology and Naval Science, he studied industrial design at the Philadelphia Museum School of Art. His professional career was marked by significant contributions to product design and accessibility, serving as the Director of Industrial Design for the Monroe Calculating Machine Company and later working with Litton Industries, Henry Dreyfuss Associates, and as an independent consultant. Hollerith was a prominent figure in the design community, serving as president of the Industrial Designers Society of America (IDSA) from 1977 to 1978 and representing the field on the President's Committee on Employment of the Handicapped. He was a pioneer in what is now known as universal design, focusing on making environments and products accessible for people with disabilities. In 1975, he received a National Endowment for the Arts (NEA) grant for projects related to the handicapped, a period during which he was active in the same professional circles as Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He established the Herman Hollerith Lecture on Innovation and Entrepreneurship at Blair Academy in 2001.
